title: Washington Drops Its Export Control Order Against Anthropic Models - TCR 07/01/26
description:
The US Commerce Department lifted all export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models three weeks after an emergency ban, a researcher used a consumer Claude Opus 4.7 subscription to gain super-admin access to the ticketing system behind many US music festivals, and Anthropic launched Claude Science as a flagship that autonomously runs computational-biology and drug-development research.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-july-1-2026/

0:00 Claude and AI browsers hand over cyber capability
0:48 Washington lifts its Anthropic model gate
4:43 The Capability the Wall Was Guarding Is in a Subscription
7:31 Australia weighs copyright for compute
10:30 Claude Science enters autonomous research
13:44 Synthesis and Closing

This episode covers the full Anthropic export-control reversal, the Front Gate Tickets super-admin flaw found through Anthropic's Cyber Verification Program, LayerX research on AI browsers bypassing their guardrails, Australia's proposed copyright-mining exemption for a $350m fund and $50bn in data centers, the Claude Science flagship launch, and capability tracking from METR, Epoch, and GDPval showing exponential progress.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick told Anthropic that no license is any longer required to export Fable or the more powerful Mythos, dissolving the June 13 emergency gate in exchange for company-run detection and safeguard protocols. Three weeks earlier the same capability was described as dangerous enough to require a wall, and open-weight GLM-5.2 already matches Mythos on bug-finding work. The reasoning behind that concern showed up plainly: Ian Carroll used Claude Opus 4.7 to find and patch a bug granting super-admin access to Front Gate Tickets in about a day, while separate research showed AI browsers coaxed into a frame where their safety rules no longer apply. Anthropic elevated Claude Science to a flagship available in beta to Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise subscribers, relocating drug-discovery reasoning out of the funded lab. Australia's cabinet weighs trading artists' consent for $50bn in compute capital, with a decision expected around July 15.
